steps.title: Recall the finite geometric series identity
steps.content: For any value of $$p$$, the product $$(1 - p)(1 + p + p^2 + \cdots + p^n)$$ telescopes to $$1 - p^{n+1}$$. Here the second factor has terms from $$p^0$$ through $$p^7$$, so $$n = 7$$ and the product equals $$1 - p^8$$.

steps.title: Verify by direct expansion
steps.content:
Multiply $$(1-p)$$ term-by-term into the series:
  • $$1 \cdot (1 + p + p^2 + p^3 + p^4 + p^5 + p^6 + p^7) = 1 + p + p^2 + p^3 + p^4 + p^5 + p^6 + p^7$$
  • $$-p \cdot (1 + p + p^2 + p^3 + p^4 + p^5 + p^6 + p^7) = -p - p^2 - p^3 - p^4 - p^5 - p^6 - p^7 - p^8$$
Adding, every middle term cancels, leaving $$1 - p^8$$.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices
steps.content:
• $1 - p^9$ - would require a factor up to $$p^8$$, not $$p^7$$. Eliminated.
  • The matching choice: $1 - p^8$ - matches the telescoped result. This is correct.
  • $1 - p^7$ - would require the series to end at $$p^6$$. Eliminated.
  • $1 - p^6$ - would require the series to end at $$p^5$$. Eliminated.
B is correct.
